V27 Session Summary

Over-stationarization fix: normalization as a dataset-family choice

Author

IndustrialJEPA

Published

April 24, 2026

One-paragraph verdict

V26’s pooled AUPRC numbers were hiding a chance-level per-horizon AUROC on C-MAPSS: the model learned the marginal P(event | Δt) but could not discriminate degradation state. Root cause is RevIN erasing the slow sensor mean drift that carries the signal (Liu+ NeurIPS 2022 “over-stationarization”). Three ablations on FD001 - remove RevIN (global z-score), NLinear-style last-value subtraction, and a stat-token variant of De-stationary Attention - show that only removing RevIN with a train-set global z-score recovers the signal. Per-horizon AUROC jumps from 0.52 → 0.64 at Δt=10 and 0.53 → 0.79 at Δt=50 on FD001, with +0.127 pooled AUPRC on FD003. The fix regresses SMAP (multi-entity anomaly) from 0.393 → 0.276 AUPRC - per-instance RevIN is doing meaningful work on multi-entity streams and must be kept there. The paper will report norm_mode as a per-dataset-family choice, not a universal recipe.

1. V26 baseline diagnostic — per-horizon AUROC, not pooled

V26 reported FD001 AUPRC = 0.925. Below is the per-horizon decomposition — the same surface, different slice.

V26 FD001 (3 seeds) — per-horizon AUROC is at chance across all Δt:
 Δt  AUROC mean  AUROC std  pred gap mean
  1       0.518      0.027         0.0004
  5       0.507      0.011         0.0055
 10       0.520      0.014         0.0063
 20       0.523      0.010         0.0069
 50       0.526      0.011         0.0048
100       0.537      0.013         0.0020
150       0.549      0.022         0.0006

V26 SMAP (3 seeds) — per-horizon AUROC is slightly above chance:
 Δt  AUROC mean  AUROC std  pred gap mean
  1       0.587      0.019         0.0587
  5       0.588      0.036         0.0668
 10       0.591      0.039         0.0665
 20       0.592      0.040         0.0648
 50       0.592      0.041         0.0631
100       0.593      0.041         0.0618
150       0.594      0.041         0.0603
200       0.594      0.041         0.0587

V26 MBA (3 seeds) — per-horizon AUROC is >0.7 at every Δt — works fine:
 Δt  AUROC mean  AUROC std  pred gap mean
  1       0.783      0.009         0.3219
  5       0.769      0.010         0.3084
 10       0.759      0.010         0.2911
 20       0.758      0.007         0.2786
 50       0.733      0.010         0.1969
100       0.712      0.007         0.0356
150       0.757      0.000         0.0078
200       0.761      0.005         0.0013

FD001’s per-horizon AUROC is 0.52 at every horizon up to Δt=100. Pooled AUPRC of 0.925 was dominated by the late-horizon base rate — at Δt=150, 99% of examples have already had the event, so AUPRC → prevalence regardless of model skill.

2. Ablation table — 4 normalization modes on FD001

variant dt=1 dt=5 dt=10 dt=20 dt=50 dt=100 dt=150 pooled AUPRC
0 v26 revin 0.518 0.507 0.520 0.523 0.526 0.537 0.549 0.9249
1 v27 none 0.622 0.623 0.639 0.674 0.789 0.860 0.857 0.9460
2 v27 last_value 0.425 0.536 0.531 0.531 0.512 0.514 0.490 0.9246
3 v27 revin_stat 0.377 0.462 0.495 0.517 0.522 0.535 0.549 0.9191

3. Per-horizon AUROC curves

4. Prediction gap diagnostic

The gap p̄(y=1) - p̄(y=0) at short Δt is the clearest signal of whether the model conditions on the input or just outputs the base rate.

5. Full benchmark: C-MAPSS FD001/2/3 (v26 vs v27 none)

dataset v26 revin AUPRC v27 none AUPRC Δ AUPRC v26 AUROC @ Δt=150 v27 AUROC @ Δt=150 Δ AUROC @ Δt=150
0 FD001 0.9249 +/- 0.0013 0.9460 +/- 0.0011 +0.0210 0.549 0.857 +0.308
1 FD002 0.9080 +/- 0.0005 0.9099 +/- 0.0001 +0.0019 0.514 0.525 +0.011
2 FD003 0.7742 +/- 0.0004 0.9013 +/- 0.0047 +0.1271 0.498 0.885 +0.387

6. Regression check — MBA + SMAP with norm_mode=‘none’

dataset v26 revin AUPRC v27 none AUPRC Δ AUPRC v26 AUROC @ Δt=1 v27 AUROC @ Δt=1 verdict
0 MBA 0.9496 +/- 0.0010 0.9461 +/- 0.0015 -0.0035 0.783 0.756 tie
1 SMAP 0.3992 +/- 0.0183 0.2758 +/- 0.0018 -0.1235 0.587 0.517 REGRESSION

SMAP regresses by 12 pp AUPRC — global z-score across 55 heterogeneous telemetry entities collapses scale information that per-instance RevIN was keeping. The paper will recommend norm_mode='revin' for multi-entity anomaly datasets.

7. FD001 engine 49 - surface heatmaps, v26 vs v27

8. MBA dense surface (v26 revin — the winner on cardiac)

9. Takeaways

  1. Pooled AUPRC can hide chance-level discrimination. V26’s FD001 AUPRC was 0.925, but per-horizon AUROC was 0.52 at Δt ≤ 100. The model was learning the marginal base-rate P(event | Δt). The per-horizon AUROC + prediction-gap diagnostic is the right primary when the base rate is non-stationary in Δt.

  2. Three normalization fixes, one winner. Removing RevIN (global z-score preprocessing) is the only tested variant that recovers the degradation signal on FD001. NLinear last-value subtraction and the stat-token approach (simplified De-stationary Attention) both stay at the v26 baseline.

  3. FD003 is the headline. +0.127 pooled AUPRC on the worst C-MAPSS subset; per-horizon AUROC at Δt=150 goes from 0.498 → 0.885.

  4. The fix is not universal. SMAP regresses by 0.117 AUPRC — the 55 telemetry entities have such heterogeneous channel scales that global z-score collapses the signal. norm_mode should be documented as a per-dataset-family choice.

  5. Two-line model change. model.py adds a single constructor argument norm_mode in {'revin', 'none', 'last_value', 'revin_stat'} that gates the pre-patch preprocessing. Default is 'revin', so v24 / v26 checkpoints load unchanged. Target encoder symmetrically mirrors the mode on every variant except 'revin_stat', which falls back to 'revin' on the target side (the stat token is asymmetric by design - it lives in the causal context only).
